planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; tolerates more open exposure than many hybrids.

directions: Amend planting soil to an acidic pH and keep the root ball high with a loose bark mulch on top. This cold-hardy Catawba hybrid is more sun-tolerant than most but still appreciates midday shade. Rounded trusses of deep red flowers open in late spring on a spreading, somewhat open plant.

planting.note: Set out in spring or early fall in dappled shade.

directions: Plant high in rich, acidic soil and protect from strong afternoon sun to keep foliage clean. Mulch and provide steady moisture, especially as buds swell. Neat conical trusses of clear light-pink flowers with a small red flare open early in the season on this dependable, dense grower.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; very cold hardy and adaptable.

directions: Set the crown slightly high in acidic, humus-enriched soil and top with a coarse organic mulch. This tough Catawba selection withstands cold and open sites better than most large hybrids. White flowers opening from lilac buds, with a light greenish-yellow throat, appear in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in filtered light.

directions: Give this large, hardy hybrid acidic soil rich in organic matter and plant it high to protect the shallow roots. Mulch well and keep moisture even through the first seasons. Big trusses of lilac-lavender flowers with a bronze-green flare open in late spring.

planting.note: Set out in spring or early fall in dappled shade.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil kept moist with a bark mulch, setting the crown just above grade. Shade from hot afternoon sun keeps the dark foliage healthy. Full trusses of rich royal-purple flowers open in mid to late spring on this old, reliable hybrid.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; hardy and vigorous.

directions: Choose acidic, organic-rich soil with good drainage and set the root ball high beneath a loose mulch. Water through dry spells and shelter from drying wind. Large trusses of bluish-purple flowers with a light rusty flare open in late spring on this robust, hardy shrub.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; very cold hardy.

directions: Plant this hardy Catawba-type hybrid high in acidic soil amended with peat or bark and mulch to keep roots cool. Provide even moisture and light afternoon shade. Big trusses of rich rose-pink flowers open in late spring on a large, spreading plant.

planting.note: Set out in spring or early fall in filtered light.

directions: Plant high in acidic, humus-rich soil that drains freely, and mulch to conserve moisture. Shade from strong afternoon sun keeps the compact habit and dense foliage attractive. Full trusses of light rose-pink flowers with a striking crimson-brown blotch open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; adaptable and sun-tolerant.

directions: Set the crown high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the shallow roots with pine bark. This vigorous, heat- and sun-tolerant hybrid makes a broad rounded shrub. Ruffled light pink flowers with a golden-bronze throat open in mid to late spring in generous quantity.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; tolerates more sun than large-leaf types.

directions: This PJM-group lepidote takes more sun and cold than large-leaf rhododendrons; give it acidic, sharply drained soil. Plant high, mulch, and avoid soggy ground. Bright clear-pink flowers smother the small aromatic leaves in early spring, and foliage turns mahogany in winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; sun-tolerant and hardy.

directions: Give this cold-hardy PJM-type lepidote acidic, well-drained soil and plant the root ball slightly high. It tolerates sun well but appreciates mulch to keep roots cool and moist. Clusters of clear pink flowers cover the plant in early spring, and the small leaves take on plum tones in cold weather.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in dappled light.

directions: Plant this low, spreading lepidote high in acidic, sharply drained soil and mulch to keep the roots cool. It stays neat and compact with little pruning. Masses of white flowers faintly spotted green open in mid spring above small, glossy leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; ideal for rock gardens and edging.

directions: This dwarf lepidote needs perfectly drained, acidic soil; plant it high and never let it sit wet. A gritty organic mulch keeps the tiny roots cool. Soft lemon-yellow flowers cover the compact mound in early spring, and the small leaves bronze in winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; superb dwarf for small spaces.

directions: Set this tidy dwarf high in gritty, acidic, free-draining soil where water never stands. Light mulch keeps the shallow roots moist and cool. Pink-and-white flowers smother the low mound so thickly in early spring the foliage nearly disappears.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in dappled shade.

directions: Plant this hardy small-leaf hybrid high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to hold moisture. It forms a broad, layered mound with little care. Creamy yellow flowers brushed with salmon-pink open in early spring, and the fine leaves turn coppery in winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; one of the hardiest yellows.

directions: Give this cold-hardy yellow hybrid acidic, humus-rich soil with sharp drainage and plant the crown high. Mulch and provide morning sun with afternoon shade for best color. Trusses of soft primrose-yellow flowers with a faint red flare open in late spring on a compact, dense plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in filtered light; milder-climate favorite.

directions: Plant high in acidic, freely draining soil enriched with bark and keep it evenly moist under mulch. Shade from hot sun protects the handsome bronze new growth. Rounded trusses of deep amber-yellow flowers emerging from orange-red buds open in mid spring on this compact hybrid.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; compact yakushimanum hybrid.

directions: This dense, dome-shaped yakushimanum hybrid wants acidic, well-drained soil and a high planting position. Mulch to keep roots cool and provide light shade. Trusses of pink flowers that fade to near-white open in mid to late spring above leaves with a soft tan felt beneath.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; very cold hardy and compact.

directions: Plant this hardy, compact hybrid high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the shallow roots. It keeps a tidy mounded form suited to smaller gardens. Trusses of ruffled reddish-purple flowers with a dark blotch open in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in dappled shade.

directions: Set the crown high in acidic, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil and shade it from harsh sun for the best flower color. Mulch and water steadily during dry weather. Trusses of lavender-blue flowers each marked with a bold dark central blotch open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; large trusses need shelter from wind.

directions: Give this award-winning hybrid acidic, well-drained soil and a wind-sheltered spot so the heavy trusses stand well. Plant high and mulch to keep the roots cool and evenly moist. Enormous domed trusses of clear pink flowers fading paler open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a hardy, felted-leaf species.

directions: Plant this hardy species high in acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter and mulch the shallow roots. The dense woolly coating on stems and leaf undersides helps it tolerate cold and sun. Trusses of rose-pink flowers open in late spring above the handsome, felted foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; excellent dwarf for rock gardens.

directions: This tiny alpine species demands gritty, sharply drained acidic soil; plant it high and keep water off the crown. A stone or grit mulch suits its rock-garden nature. Small violet-blue flowers cover the low, twiggy mound of gray-green scaly leaves in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; prostrate creeper for rock gardens.

directions: Plant this prostrate dwarf on a slope or in a trough with gritty, acidic, free-draining soil so it never stands wet. Keep it high with a light grit mulch. Pale lemon-yellow flowers appear in early spring over a low carpet of tiny leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; grown as much for foliage as flower.

directions: Give this distinctive species acidic, well-drained soil and plant it high beneath a bark mulch. It is prized for its very narrow leaves clad in thick tawny felt below and on the new shoots. Trusses of soft pink flowers open in late spring, later than most rhododendrons.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in dappled light.

directions: Plant this upright lepidote species high in acidic, sharply drained soil and mulch to keep the roots cool. It flowers along the length of its slender stems rather than in single terminal trusses. Small pink flowers line the branches profusely in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a hardy alpine species.

directions: Plant this low mountain species in gritty, acidic, free-draining soil and keep the crown high and dry. It suits cool climates and rock gardens where drainage is perfect. Clusters of rosy-red flowers open in early summer above small leaves rust-colored with scales beneath.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; native to the southern Appalachians.

directions: Plant this native deciduous azalea high in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil and mulch to keep the shallow roots moist. It blooms after the leaves expand, later than most native species. Clusters of orange to red flowers open in early summer on a rounded, twiggy shrub.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; the fragrant azalea of the West Coast.

directions: Give this fragrant native azalea acidic, moisture-retentive soil that still drains, and plant it high beneath a deep mulch. It naturally grows along streambanks and tolerates more moisture than many azaleas. Sweetly scented white to pale-pink flowers marked with a yellow flare open in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; one of the hardiest azaleas.

directions: Plant this exceptionally hardy azalea high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the fibrous roots. It holds some leaves in mild winters and drops them where cold is severe. Fragrant rosy-lilac flowers cover the low, spreading plant in mid spring, often before the leaves fully expand.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; the wild parent of many Kurume azaleas.

directions: Plant this dense dwarf species high in acidic, sharply drained soil and mulch to keep the fine roots cool. Its naturally low, twiggy habit suits rock gardens and foreground plantings. Small flowers in pink to purple shades smother the compact mound in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; key parent of the Mollis hybrids.

directions: Plant this deciduous species high in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil and mulch to conserve moisture. It appreciates cooler summers and shelter from hot afternoon sun. Fragrant orange, salmon, or red flowers open in mid to late spring before or as the leaves unfold.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; extremely cold-hardy Northern Lights series.

directions: This Minnesota-bred deciduous azalea survives severe cold; plant it high in acidic, well-drained soil with plenty of organic matter. Mulch the roots and keep moisture even. Fragrant flowers open pale pink and fade to white, marked with a yellow blotch, in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; dwarf, very hardy Northern Lights selection.

directions: Plant this compact, ultra-hardy azalea high in acidic, well-drained soil rich in humus and mulch well. Its low mounded habit suits small beds and cold-climate gardens. Clusters of light lilac-purple flowers open in mid to late spring on the tidy plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; hardy Northern Lights series.

directions: Plant this cold-hardy deciduous azalea high in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil and mulch to hold moisture. Give it good light for the fullest bloom while sheltering from scorching afternoon sun. Fragrant lemon-yellow flowers open in mid to late spring on an upright, vigorous plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a classic Exbury hybrid.

directions: Plant this large Exbury azalea high in acidic, well-drained soil enriched with organic matter and mulch the roots. Give it good light for heavy flowering with some relief from hot sun. Large trusses of salmon-pink flowers with a yellow-orange flare open in mid to late spring before the leaves fully expand.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a vivid Exbury hybrid.

directions: Plant this Exbury azalea high in acidic, humus-rich soil with sharp drainage and mulch to keep the roots cool. The bronze-red new foliage sets off the bloom. Trusses of deep orange-red flowers open in mid to late spring on an upright, vigorous shrub.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; an Exbury hybrid with colorful new growth.

directions: Give this Exbury azalea acidic, well-drained soil enriched with organic matter and plant it high beneath a mulch. Its coppery new leaves complement the flowers handsomely. Fragrant rose-pink flowers with a yellow flare open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a large Southern Indica azalea.

directions: Plant this large evergreen Southern Indica azalea high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the fibrous roots. It thrives in the warm Southeast with light shade. Big pure-white flowers cover the tall, broad shrub in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a large Southern Indica azalea.

directions: Set this vigorous Southern Indica azalea high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to conserve moisture in warm climates. It makes a large landscape shrub in light shade. Big watermelon-pink flowers, sometimes flecked deeper, open in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a Southern Indica azalea for mild climates.

directions: Plant this Southern Indica azalea high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the shallow roots for the warm Southeast. Provide light shade to protect the flowers from scorch. Large white flowers with a faint chartreuse throat and slightly frilled edges open in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a compact Kurume azalea.

directions: Plant this dense Kurume azalea high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the fine roots. Its tight, twiggy habit responds well to a light shearing after bloom. Small bright rose-red flowers cover the compact plant in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a cold-hardy Gable azalea.

directions: Plant this hardy Gable azalea high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the shallow roots. Its low, spreading habit makes a fine massing or foundation plant in cold gardens. Frilled reddish-purple flowers with a darker blotch smother the plant in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a fragrant, hardy Gable azalea.

directions: Plant this low, spreading Gable azalea high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to keep the roots cool. Its dense habit suits the front of beds and foundations. Fragrant white flowers with a soft green-yellow throat open in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a low Satsuki azalea that blooms late.

directions: Plant this dwarf Satsuki azalea high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the shallow roots. Its low, mounding habit is ideal for edging and rock gardens. Large ruffled white flowers open in late spring, well after most azaleas have finished.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a Robin Hill azalea with variable flowers.

directions: Plant this spreading Robin Hill azalea high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the fine roots. It blooms a bit later and tolerates more sun than Kurume types. Large flowers in mixed shades of pink, white, and rose, some striped or blotched, appear on the same plant in late spring.

planting.note: Set out in autumn so roots settle before the first spring flush.

directions: Dig a broad hole and plant with the root crown slightly proud of the surrounding grade, since camellias resent deep planting. Mulch to keep the shallow roots cool and water deeply through summer to build next winter's buds. This sport-prone variety blooms white to pink streaked and blotched with rose from midwinter into spring.

planting.note: Plant in cool weather into acidic, humus-rich ground.

directions: Site in dappled shade sheltered from harsh morning sun that can scorch frozen buds. Keep the soil evenly moist and feed with an acid fertilizer after flowering finishes. Semi-double blooms open white striped with rose-pink, often varying from branch to branch on the same bush.

planting.note: Fall planting lets this vigorous old variety root before bloom.

directions: Plant high in loose, well-drained acidic soil and never let it dry out during bud set in late summer. A dependable, upright grower that tolerates more sun than many japonicas once established. Rose-pink peony-to-rose-form flowers arrive from midseason into spring.

planting.note: An early bloomer worth siting where its winter flowers are visible.

directions: Choose a protected spot in partial shade because this variety opens very early and blooms can be frost-nipped in cold snaps. Set the plant with its crown at or just above grade and mulch generously. Large rose-pink peony-form flowers splashed with white appear from early winter onward.

planting.note: Plant in autumn into acidic, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Give it filtered light and steady moisture, and keep the shallow roots mulched year-round. Prune lightly just after flowering to shape without sacrificing next season's buds. Large red semi-double blooms with fringed, white-marked petals carry through the midwinter-to-spring season.

planting.note: A late bloomer that dodges most hard freezes when planted in fall.

directions: Plant high in rich acidic soil and provide afternoon shade in hot regions. This compact, bushy grower tolerates heat well, making it popular in the Deep South. Deep salmon-red semi-double flowers bloom from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Widely sold as Glen 40; plant in cool weather for best establishment.

directions: Set out in partial shade with the crown slightly raised and mulch to conserve moisture. Its slow, upright, compact habit makes it a favorite for containers and formal beds. Deep red formal-double blooms of exhibition quality open from midseason into spring.

planting.note: Vigorous grower; give it room and autumn planting.

directions: Allow generous spacing for this fast, open grower and plant high in acidic, well-drained soil. Water consistently and shelter from drying wind to protect the very large blooms. Rose-pink anemone-to-semi-double flowers with a showy boss of stamens appear from early to midseason.

planting.note: Plant into rich acidic soil in the cool season.

directions: Give filtered light and even moisture, keeping roots mulched and never waterlogged. A compact, dense, upright bush that is easy for beginners. Pale pink semi-double flowers shading to deeper pink at the petal edges open from midseason into spring.

planting.note: Old cultivar also known as Pope Pius IX; plant in fall.

directions: Plant high in acidic, humus-rich soil and mulch to protect the surface roots. Prune lightly after bloom to keep the upright form tidy. Cherry-red formal-double flowers of classic camellia symmetry open from midseason to late season.

planting.note: Vigorous variety with very large flowers; plant in autumn.

directions: Give this robust grower room and steady moisture to support its huge blooms. Plant high in acidic soil, mulch well, and shelter from wind. Blush-pink semi-double flowers, among the largest of any japonica, appear from early to midseason.

planting.note: Plant in cool weather into moist, acidic ground.

directions: Site in dappled shade and keep evenly moist through the dry season to set full buds. Mulch the shallow roots and feed lightly with an acid fertilizer after flowering. Soft orchid-pink semi-double blooms with ruffled petals open from midseason into spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn into rich, acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Choose partial shade and keep the root zone cool and moist beneath a layer of mulch. Its vigorous, upright habit responds well to light shaping after bloom. Large white single flowers with wavy petals and golden stamens open from early to midseason.

planting.note: Compact, cold-tolerant japonica good for smaller gardens.

directions: Plant with the crown high and mulch to protect roots through summer heat and winter cold. Its dense, compact form suits foundation plantings and hedges. Cream-to-pale-pink peony-form flowers edged in deeper pink open from midseason to late season.

planting.note: Also known as Mikenjaku; plant in fall into acidic soil.

directions: Give this spreading, vigorous variety plenty of room and plant high in loose acidic soil. Water deeply during bud set and mulch to keep roots cool. Large rose-pink semi-double flowers marbled with white appear from midseason into spring.

planting.note: Late-flowering white japonica; plant in the cool season.

directions: Plant in filtered light where late frosts are unlikely to brown the white petals. Keep the acidic soil evenly moist and mulched, and prune only to shape after bloom. Pure white rose-form-to-formal-double flowers open from mid to late season.

planting.note: Early-blooming white; site out of morning sun.

directions: Plant high in rich acidic soil and shelter from early sun that can scorch frosted white blooms. Maintain steady moisture and a cooling mulch over the shallow roots. Large white semi-double flowers with a central cluster of stamens open early to midseason.

planting.note: Old formal-double variety; plant into acidic soil in fall.

directions: Set the crown slightly high in well-drained acidic soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Its slow, dense growth suits formal borders and large containers. Rose-red formal-double flowers veined in deeper red open from mid to late season.

planting.note: Vigorous reticulata hybrid; give it space and autumn planting.

directions: Allow generous room for this fast, upright grower and plant high in well-drained acidic soil. Water deeply and shelter from wind to protect the very large flowers. Bright red-to-rose semi-double blooms up to seven inches across open from early to midseason.

planting.note: Free-flowering williamsii hybrid that drops spent blooms cleanly.

directions: Plant high in acidic, moisture-retentive soil in dappled shade or morning sun. This tough, upright hybrid flowers heavily even for beginners and sheds faded blooms on its own. Prolific orchid-pink semi-double flowers cover the plant from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Upright williamsii hybrid excellent for narrow spaces.

directions: Site in partial shade and plant with the crown just above grade in rich acidic soil. Its compact, columnar habit fits tight garden corners and large pots. Deep rose peony-form flowers appear over a long midwinter-to-spring season.

planting.note: Formal-double williamsii hybrid; plant in the cool season.

directions: Plant high in acidic soil with dappled light and keep the roots cool under mulch. The upright, dense form needs little pruning beyond shaping after bloom. Symmetrical lavender-pink formal-double flowers resembling a water lily open from mid to late season.

planting.note: Reliable, cold-tolerant williamsii hybrid.

directions: Plant in morning sun or light shade in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the roots. A vigorous, free-flowering hybrid that performs in cooler regions. Bright rose-pink peony-form flowers cover the bush from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Upright, formal-double williamsii hybrid; plant in fall.

directions: Set out in partial shade and plant high in humus-rich acidic soil. Its neat, upright habit makes it ideal for screens and formal beds. Soft pink formal-double flowers of tidy symmetry open from mid to late season.

planting.note: The original williamsii hybrid; long-flowering and hardy.

directions: Plant high in acidic soil in dappled shade, allowing space for its loose, spreading branches. This durable hybrid flowers over many months and tolerates cool climates. Clear pink single flowers with golden stamens appear from early winter into spring.

planting.note: Upright, glossy-leaved williamsii hybrid.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in light shade and mulch to keep the roots cool. The compact, upright form and bronze new growth make it handsome year-round. Rose-pink semi-double flowers open freely from midwinter into spring.

planting.note: Cup-shaped single williamsii hybrid; hardy and early.

directions: Site in morning sun or filtered light and plant high in moist acidic soil. Its upright habit and early bloom brighten the late-winter garden. Rose-pink funnel-shaped single flowers open from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Compact hybrid with bell-shaped blooms; long season.

directions: Plant high in acidic soil in partial shade and keep evenly moist through the growing season. Its dense, bushy habit suits hedges, containers, and small gardens. Bright red bell-shaped semi-double flowers appear over an unusually long midwinter-to-spring season.

planting.note: Reticulata-saluenensis hybrid; upright and free-flowering.

directions: Plant high in well-drained acidic soil in dappled light and mulch the shallow roots. This vigorous, upright hybrid can be espaliered against a wall. Deep pink semi-double flowers appear in profusion over a long late-winter-to-spring season.

planting.note: Historic reticulata; needs a sheltered, frost-free site.

directions: Grow in a warm, sheltered position or a cool greenhouse in cold-winter areas, as reticulatas are frost-sensitive. Plant high in gritty acidic soil and give it room for its open, tree-like frame. Very large carmine-rose semi-double flowers open in late winter.

planting.note: Reticulata hybrid; vigorous and good for espalier.

directions: Plant in a sheltered spot with well-drained acidic soil and allow room for its long, arching branches. It trains readily against a warm wall where winters are mild. Large rose-pink wavy semi-double flowers open from early to midseason.

planting.note: Large-flowered reticulata; needs a mild, sheltered site.

directions: Site where winters stay mild and plant high in gritty, well-drained acidic soil. Water deeply during dry spells and shelter the large blooms from wind. Enormous red flowers, variable from semi-double to peony form, open from early to midseason.

planting.note: Formal-double reticulata hybrid for mild climates.

directions: Plant in a warm, protected position in well-drained acidic soil and mulch generously. Its upright, vigorous frame benefits from light shaping when young. Large salmon-red formal-double flowers of near-perfect symmetry open from early to midseason.

planting.note: Reticulata hybrid with very large soft-pink blooms.

directions: Grow in a sheltered, mild-winter site and plant high in gritty acidic soil. Feed after flowering and protect the large blooms from cold wind and rain. Very large soft-pink semi-double flowers with heavy petals open from early to midseason.

planting.note: Sun-tolerant sasanqua; plant in fall for autumn bloom.

directions: Unlike japonicas, this sasanqua tolerates full sun once established in well-drained acidic soil. Plant high, mulch the roots, and train as a loose hedge, screen, or espalier. Single pink flowers open across the plant in fall and early winter.

planting.note: Low, spreading sasanqua ideal for espalier or groundcover.

directions: Plant in sun to light shade in well-drained acidic soil and use its low, arching habit for banks or wall training. Mulch the roots and water through establishment. Soft pink semi-double flowers, sometimes marbled white, open in fall and early winter.

planting.note: Also sold as Christmas Beauty; blooms early winter.

directions: Plant in sun to partial shade in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to keep roots cool. This vigorous, upright grower tolerates more sun than japonicas and takes well to hedging. Rose-pink semi-double flowers open from fall into early winter.

planting.note: Cold-tolerant fall bloomer good for the upper South.

directions: Plant high in well-drained acidic soil in sun or light shade and mulch the shallow roots. Its upright, hardy habit suits screens and foundation plantings in colder camellia zones. Blush-white single-to-semi-double flowers tinged pink open from fall into early winter.

planting.note: Fragrant sasanqua; plant in fall for autumn flowers.

directions: Set out in sun to light shade in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Its upright habit and lightly fragrant blooms make it a good specimen near paths. White single flowers shaded rose-pink on the reverse open in fall and early winter.

planting.note: Cold-hardy oleifera hybrid from the Winter series.

directions: Plant high in well-drained acidic soil in sun or partial shade; this hybrid survives colder winters than most camellias. Mulch the roots and shelter from harsh wind in exposed sites. White anemone-form flowers open from fall into early winter.

planting.note: The hardiest camellia species and parent of many cold-hardy hybrids.

directions: Plant high in well-drained acidic soil in sun or light shade; this species is prized for its cold hardiness and peeling bark. Water through establishment and mulch the shallow roots. Fragrant white single flowers open in fall, followed by seed capsules used for oil.

planting.note: Rare yellow-flowered species needing warm, humid, frost-free conditions.

directions: Grow in warm, humid shade with consistently moist, acidic, well-drained soil, or under glass in cooler climates. Protect entirely from frost, as this tropical species is cold-tender. Waxy golden-yellow cupped flowers, unusual among camellias, open in winter.

planting.note: Set out established plants in spring once the ground has warmed and settled.

directions: Plant the crown two inches below the soil line at the base of a large arbor or fence it can quickly cover. This early bloomer flowers on last year's wood, so prune only lightly right after the spring flush fades. Expect a sheet of small pale-pink to white flowers in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its long season of nodding bells will show against a wall.

directions: Give this vigorous grower a sturdy support and set the crown a couple inches below grade. It flowers on new growth, so cut the whole plant back hard to about a foot in late winter. Yellow lantern-shaped flowers carry from midsummer into fall, followed by silky silver seed heads.

planting.note: Plant in spring with the roots shaded and the top in sun.

directions: Bury the crown two inches deep in rich, evenly moist soil and mulch to keep the roots cool. As a late-blooming type, shear it back to about eighteen inches in early spring. Velvety crimson blooms open from summer through early fall on the current year's shoots.

planting.note: Plant in spring near a trellis or container obelisk where the showy flowers can be admired up close.

directions: Set the crown two inches below the surface in fertile, well-drained soil and keep the base cool with mulch or low plants. Prune lightly in early spring, removing only dead stems, since it blooms on both old and new wood. The large lilac-pink double rosettes hold for weeks from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a trellis in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: Plant deep with the crown two inches under the soil and shade the roots with mulch. Prune only lightly in early spring to preserve the old wood that carries the first flush. Double violet-blue rosettes appear in late spring, followed by single flowers later in the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the vivid red flowers will catch the eye.

directions: Set the crown two inches below grade and keep the roots cool and evenly moist. Cut stems back to about eighteen inches in early spring, as flowers form on new growth. Large scarlet blooms with a deeper central bar appear from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a spot with morning sun to keep the pink bars from fading.

directions: Plant deep with two inches of soil over the crown and mulch to shade the roots. Prune lightly in early spring, taking out only weak or dead stems. Deep pink flowers with a carmine central band open in late spring and repeat later in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the brightest crimson color.

directions: Set the crown two inches deep in fertile soil and keep the root zone cool with mulch. Cut the plant back to roughly eighteen inches in early spring since it flowers on new wood. Large glowing crimson blooms carry from early summer well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring to scramble through a shrub or over a fence.

directions: Plant the crown two inches below the soil and mulch to keep the roots shaded. As a late-flowering type, shear it to about eighteen inches in early spring. Wavy-edged sky-blue flowers bloom generously from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a trellis, arbor, or through a large shrub.

directions: Set the crown two inches below grade in rich, moisture-retentive soil and mulch the base. Cut all stems back to about eighteen inches each spring for the strongest new flowering wood. Broad velvety purple flowers, fuller than the classic Jackmanii, cover the vine from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a trellis in sun to light shade.

directions: Plant deep with two inches of soil over the crown and keep the roots cool. Prune lightly in early spring, cutting back only to a strong pair of buds. Large deep lavender-blue flowers with wavy petals appear in late spring and again in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring to weave through roses or over a fence.

directions: Bury the crown two inches deep and mulch to shade the roots of this tough, disease-resistant vine. Cut it hard to about a foot in early spring, since flowers come on new growth. Small nodding wine-red flowers appear in profusion from midsummer into early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its quirky flowers can ramble through dark foliage.

directions: Set the crown two inches below the soil line and mulch the base well. Cut the whole plant back to about a foot in late winter for vigorous fresh growth. White flowers, often green-tipped and irregular, nod along the stems from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring on an obelisk or through a climbing rose.

directions: Plant the crown two inches deep and keep the root zone cool with mulch. Prune this new-wood bloomer hard to about a foot in early spring. Twisted wine-red petals with paler stamens open freely from midsummer to early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the many small double flowers can be seen at close range.

directions: Bury the crown two inches below grade and mulch to shade the roots. Cut the plant hard to about a foot in early spring for strong new flowering shoots. Dense dusky-purple double rosettes, which set no seed, cover the vine from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring to scramble through shrubs or over a fence in sun.

directions: Set the crown two inches deep and keep the base cool and moist under mulch. Prune to about a foot in early spring, as it blooms on the current season's growth. Small nodding crimson flowers appear in abundance from midsummer into early autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the veined bicolor flowers can be enjoyed up close.

directions: Plant the crown two inches below the soil and mulch the roots. Cut the vine back hard to about a foot in early spring for fresh flowering wood. Striking white flowers heavily veined and edged in purple open from midsummer to early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a trellis or through a supporting shrub.

directions: Set the crown two inches below grade and keep the roots shaded with mulch. Prune hard to about a foot in early spring since flowers form on new growth. Upfacing white flowers rimmed with a broad band of mauve-purple bloom from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring beside an obelisk or shrub the non-clinging stems can lean on.

directions: Set the crown two inches deep and tie the stems in, as this hybrid does not cling on its own. Cut it back to about a foot in early spring since it flowers on new growth. Nodding bell-shaped flowers of deep indigo-purple appear from early summer well into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a border where it can scramble over low neighbors.

directions: Plant the crown two inches deep and let this non-clinging herbaceous type sprawl or lean on supports. Cut all stems to the ground in late winter, as it dies back and reblooms from the base. Flat blue-mauve flowers with cream stamens carry from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring next to a shrub or trellis the leaning stems can be tied to.

directions: Set the crown two inches deep and support the stems, which cannot climb by themselves. Cut the plant back to about a foot in early spring for fresh flowering shoots. Large nodding indigo-blue flowers with prominent cream stamens bloom from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the front or middle of a sunny border.

directions: Plant this non-climbing shrubby clematis at the same depth it grew and give it room to form a mound. Cut the old stems back near the ground in late winter, as it flowers on new growth. Clusters of small tubular blue flowers with recurved lobes are lightly fragrant in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a border where the tall stems can be staked or left to lean.

directions: Set this upright, non-climbing perennial at the depth it grew and stake it early, as the tall stems tend to flop. Cut all growth to the ground in late winter for fresh shoots each year. Airy clouds of small, fragrant white star flowers appear in early summer above the foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the tulip-shaped flowers can be seen against a trellis.

directions: Plant the crown two inches deep in well-drained soil and keep the roots cool with mulch. Cut it back to about a foot in early spring since it blooms on new wood. Bright pink upfacing tulip-shaped flowers appear from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a low trellis or through shrubs in full sun.

directions: Set the crown two inches below grade in gritty, well-drained soil and mulch the base. Prune hard to about a foot in early spring for the strongest new flowering wood. Nodding pink tulip-shaped bells with a deeper central band appear from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, well-drained spot with good air movement.

directions: Plant the crown two inches deep and keep the roots cool while the top gets full sun. Cut the vine back hard to about a foot in early spring, as flowers form on new growth. Glowing ruby-red tulip-shaped flowers open widely and carry from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the nodding bells can be viewed from below.

directions: Set the crown two inches deep in well-drained soil and mulch to shade the roots. Cut the plant back to about a foot in early spring since it flowers on new wood. Nodding cherry-pink bells with silvery margins hang along the stems from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a fence or trellis for an early spring display.

directions: Plant the crown two inches deep and let this hardy early type climb a support. It flowers on old wood, so prune only lightly right after blooming to shape the plant. Nodding semi-double pink bells appear in mid-spring, followed by fluffy seed heads.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its early bells will greet the season on a trellis.

directions: Set the crown two inches below grade and give this very hardy vine a support to climb. Prune only lightly after the spring flowers fade, since it blooms on old wood. Nodding semi-double lavender-blue bells open in mid to late spring, with fluffy seed heads to follow.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a trellis or through an early shrub for spring flowers.

directions: Plant the crown two inches deep and let this compact, hardy vine scramble up a support. It blooms on old wood, so prune only lightly after flowering to tidy it. Nodding blue lantern flowers with cream centers appear in mid-spring, followed by silky seed heads.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a small trellis or container obelisk.

directions: Set the crown two inches deep and give this compact alpine type a light support. Prune only lightly right after the spring flush, since it blooms on the previous year's wood. Nodding semi-double reddish-pink bells appear in mid-spring, followed by attractive seed heads.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot against a warm wall in mild climates.

directions: Plant this evergreen clematis at a sheltered, well-drained site with the crown two inches deep. Prune only lightly after the winter flowers finish, as it blooms on old wood. Nodding cream bells heavily speckled with maroon inside open from late fall through winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a warm wall where winter bloom can be appreciated.

directions: Set the crown two inches deep in free-draining soil in a protected, sunny location. Trim lightly after the winter flowering to keep the evergreen growth in bounds. Nodding, unmarked creamy-white bells appear through the coldest months over glossy foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a compact trellis or in a large container.

directions: Plant the crown two inches deep and keep the base cool and moist with mulch. Prune lightly in early spring, removing only dead stems, since it flowers on old and new wood. Deep rose-pink flowers with paler centers open early and repeat later in the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a trellis in sun to light shade.

directions: Set the crown two inches deep in fertile soil and shade the roots with mulch. Prune lightly in early spring, cutting to a strong pair of buds. Large soft sky-blue flowers with cream stamens open in early summer and repeat in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a trellis or fence in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant the crown two inches deep and keep the root zone cool with mulch. Prune lightly in early spring, taking out only weak or dead wood. Large lavender-blue flowers with overlapping wavy petals bloom in early summer and again in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the double flowers can be seen at close range.

directions: Set the crown two inches deep and mulch the roots to keep them cool. Prune only lightly in early spring to preserve the old wood that carries the double flowers. Full mauve-pink rosettes open in early summer, with single flowers following later in the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a trellis in a sunny, sheltered position.

directions: Plant the crown two inches deep and keep the roots cool beneath mulch. Prune lightly in early spring so the old wood can produce the first semi-double flowers. Large deep violet-purple blooms with creamy stamens appear in early summer, then single flowers later.

planting.note: Plant in spring to cover a fence, arbor, or large shrub.

directions: Set the crown two inches deep in rich soil and mulch to shade the roots. Cut the plant back to about eighteen inches in early spring, as it blooms on new growth. Deep reddish-purple flowers with a bright carmine bar appear from midsummer into early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun where the vigorous stems can climb high.

directions: Plant the crown two inches deep and give this strong grower a large support in full sun. Cut it hard to about eighteen inches in early spring for abundant new flowering wood. Pearly white flowers, mauve-banded on the reverse, cover the vine from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, sunny spot to ensure the late flowers ripen.

directions: Set the crown two inches deep and choose the sunniest site so this late bloomer flowers before frost. Cut the vine back to about eighteen inches in early spring. Rich violet-blue flowers with cream stamens open from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny position for reliable late flowers.

directions: Plant the crown two inches deep and give this vigorous late variety full sun and a tall support. Cut it back to about eighteen inches in early spring, as flowers form on new growth. Soft lilac-rose flowers appear from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a trellis or through a supporting shrub.

directions: Set the crown two inches deep and mulch the roots to keep them cool. Cut the stems back to about eighteen inches in early spring for fresh flowering shoots. Bright azure-blue flowers with wavy-edged petals bloom freely from midsummer into early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the double flowers can be seen up close on a trellis.

directions: Plant the crown two inches deep and keep the roots cool and shaded with mulch. Prune only lightly in early spring, since the fully double flowers form on old wood. Silvery mauve rosettes open in early summer over neat foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring only where its vigorous growth can be managed, as it can be invasive.

directions: Give this very vigorous European species a large, robust structure and plant the crown two inches deep. Cut it back hard in late winter to keep the rampant growth in check. Clouds of small fragrant creamy-white flowers in late summer give way to the fluffy silver seed heads that name it.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring or early fall while soil is workable.

directions: Plant the crown so the point where roots meet foliage sits about an inch below the soil surface. This creamy miniature carries a bold purple eye and blooms in early to midsummer, reblooming in warm regions. Water steadily through the first season and divide clumps every three to four years to keep flowering strong.

planting.note: Best planted in spring or fall in loose, well-drained ground.

directions: Space the small clumps a foot and a half apart and firm soil around the roots after planting. This early-blooming orange miniature produces many small ruffled flowers and often flushes again later in the season. Deadhead spent scapes and give a spring feeding to encourage repeat bloom.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring or early autumn.

directions: Tuck this compact selection into the front of a border with the crown just below grade. Its deep grape-purple flowers open in early to midsummer on short scapes ideal for edging and containers. Keep evenly moist during establishment and remove old foliage in late winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set plants two feet apart with roots spread over a low mound of soil. This tall cultivar bears large cream-and-lavender blended blooms with a green throat in midsummer. Mulch lightly to conserve moisture and lift crowded clumps every few years for continued vigor.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Position the crown an inch below the surface and water in well. Bright red flowers with a yellow-green throat appear over a long midsummer season on sturdy scapes. Feed each spring and split large clumps to keep the flower count high.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant clumps two feet apart with the crown just below soil level. Large near-white flowers carry a striking dark purple eye and picotee edge through mid to late summer, with rebloom in warm zones. Keep soil evenly moist and remove faded scapes to tidy the plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall for summer flowering.

directions: Firm the soil around spread roots so the crown rests an inch deep. Deep velvety purple flowers with a sharp yellow-green throat glow on tall scapes from midsummer onward and rebloom reliably. Water during dry spells and divide every three to four years.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall.

directions: Set the crown just below grade and water thoroughly at planting. Small lemon-yellow flowers backed with mahogany open freely on branching wiry scapes through midsummer. This vigorous older selection tolerates poor soil but rewards a spring feeding with heavier bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Spread the roots over a firmed cone of soil and keep the crown near the surface. Many small golden-yellow flowers with reddish-brown backs dangle from tall, well-branched scapes in early to midsummer. Give steady moisture and divide crowded clumps to sustain the airy display.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn.

directions: Plant this miniature at the front of a bed with the crown an inch below the soil. Small wine-red flowers with a green throat appear over a long early-to-mid season on slender scapes. Water regularly through establishment and lift clumps every few years to renew flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Set the small crown just beneath the surface and firm the soil around it. Soft pearly pink flowers with a green throat open in profusion during early to midsummer on compact scapes. Deadhead to keep the display neat and divide every three years to maintain bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall for a long bloom season.

directions: Space these dwarf plants about a foot apart with the crown level in the soil. Small golden-yellow flowers repeat from early summer into fall, making it a fine edger and container plant. Keep moist and shear spent scapes to encourage continued flushes of bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall.

directions: Plant the crown an inch below grade and water in at planting. Rounded orchid-pink flowers with a bold purple eye and green throat appear in midsummer on tidy scapes. This compact selection suits the border front; divide every few years to keep clumps productive.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set roots over a firmed mound and keep the crown near the surface. Spidery pale yellow flowers with narrow segments and a green throat open in midsummer on strong scapes. Water through dry weather and divide crowded clumps to preserve the graceful form.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; very adaptable.

directions: Plant this tough old cultivar with the crown just below the soil surface. Rusty brick-red flowers with a yellow throat cover the clump in midsummer and multiply quickly. It thrives with little fuss; divide the vigorous clumps every three years to share or spread.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall.

directions: Water the crown in well after setting it an inch below grade. Rich cherry-red flowers with a lighter watermark and green throat bloom through midsummer. Feed in spring, keep evenly moist, and remove spent scapes to keep the clump looking fresh.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown just below the surface over spread roots. Fully double, deep golden-yellow flowers give a peony-like look through mid to late summer. Keep soil moist during bloom and divide every few years to maintain a strong flower count.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn.

directions: Plant two feet apart with the crown near the soil surface. Ruffled double flowers in clear golden yellow open in midsummer on tall scapes above healthy foliage. Water steadily and lift crowded clumps every three to four years for reliable rebloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Spread roots over firmed soil and keep the crown an inch deep. Large spider-form flowers in deep red with a yellow-green throat carry long, twisting segments in midsummer. Give steady moisture and divide every few years to keep the dramatic blooms coming.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall.

directions: Set the crown just below grade and water in at planting. This classic spider bears very long, narrow bright yellow segments that twist and cascade in midsummer. Support is unnecessary, but keep soil moist and divide every few years to renew the clump.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in fertile soil.

directions: Firm the soil around spread roots so the crown sits an inch deep. Small, near-black red flowers with a green throat show off on tall, well-branched scapes in midsummer. Water regularly and divide crowded clumps every three to four years for best display.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant the crown just below grade and water thoroughly. Warm brown-toned flowers with a golden throat give an unusual color in the midsummer border. Keep evenly moist, feed each spring, and remove old scapes to keep the clump tidy.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set roots over firmed soil with the crown near the surface. Large fragrant butter-yellow flowers bloom over a long midsummer season on tall scapes. Water during dry spells and divide every few years to sustain the abundant, sweetly scented display.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant the crown an inch below grade and water in well. Light orange-yellow flowers with a deep maroon eye open in midsummer on tall, branching scapes. This dependable older cultivar spreads readily; divide every three to four years to keep it vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown just below the surface over spread roots. Ruffled melon-and-rose blended flowers with a green throat open in midsummer on sturdy scapes. Keep evenly moist through bloom and divide crowded clumps every few years.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall for a long bloom season.

directions: Plant this compact rebloomer with the crown level in the soil. Ruffled apricot-orange flowers repeat from early summer into fall, ideal for edges and containers. Deadhead and keep moist to encourage the strongest repeat flushes.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall.

directions: Water the crown in well after setting it an inch below grade. Rich purple flowers with a paler watermark, gold edge, and green throat bloom in midsummer and often rebloom. Feed each spring and divide every three to four years to keep flowering heavy.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown just below the surface over firmed soil. Deep rust-red flowers with a bold darker eye and gold edge open in midsummer on strong scapes. Keep evenly moist during bloom and divide crowded clumps every few years.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant the crown an inch below grade and water in well. Pink flowers with a bright red eye and green throat bloom in midsummer and rebloom reliably. Feed in spring, keep moist, and deadhead to keep the clump flowering over a long season.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Set this compact selection with the crown just below the surface. Soft pink flowers with a rose-pink eye and green throat open in early to midsummer on tidy scapes. Keep moist during establishment and divide every three years to maintain bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall.

directions: Plant the crown near the surface over spread roots and water in. Purple flowers with a lighter watermark and green throat bloom in mid to late summer on sturdy scapes. Keep evenly moist and divide crowded clumps every few years for best flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Set the crown an inch below grade and firm the soil around it. Golden-yellow flowers with a dark maroon eye and green throat open in midsummer on compact scapes. This tidy selection is fine for the border front; divide every few years to renew.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall.

directions: Plant the crown just below the surface and water thoroughly. Deep wine-purple flowers with a lighter watermark and green throat bloom in midsummer on strong scapes. Keep moist through bloom, feed each spring, and divide every few years.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set roots over firmed soil with the crown near the surface. Fully double, ruffled clear yellow flowers give a peony-like effect in midsummer and often rebloom. Water during dry spells and divide crowded clumps every three to four years.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant the crown an inch below grade and water in well. Soft peach-cream flowers blushed pink with a green throat open in midsummer on well-branched scapes. Keep evenly moist and divide every few years to keep the delicate blooms plentiful.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown near the surface over spread roots and water in. Cream-and-lavender patterned flowers with an intricate eye and green throat open in midsummer. Keep moist through bloom and divide crowded clumps every few years to sustain the display.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall; mulch in colder zones.

directions: Plant the crown an inch below grade and water well. Ruffled lavender-pink flowers with a bold purple eye and gold edge bloom in midsummer. This evergreen selection prefers warmer regions; keep moist and mulch the crown where winters are cold.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Set the crown just below the surface over firmed soil. Narrow spider-form flowers open chartreuse-yellow with a strong green throat in early to midsummer. Keep evenly moist and divide every few years to maintain the unusual green-toned bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall.

directions: Plant the crown an inch below grade and water in well. Bright true-red flowers with a small green throat glow in midsummer on sturdy scapes. Feed in spring, keep moist, and divide crowded clumps every three to four years for heavy bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Spread roots over firmed soil and keep the crown near the surface. Large spider flowers in gold with a rose-red eyezone carry long, narrow segments on tall scapes in midsummer. Give steady moisture and divide every few years to keep the striking form.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall.

directions: Set the crown an inch deep over spread roots and water in. Spidery deep purple flowers with a paler watermark and green throat twist open in midsummer on tall scapes. Keep evenly moist and divide crowded clumps every few years for best flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Plant the crown just below grade and water thoroughly. Soft pastel yellow flowers with a green throat open over a long midsummer season on branching scapes. Keep moist through bloom and divide every three to four years to renew the clump.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Set this compact plant with the crown level in the soil. Small cranberry-red flowers with a green throat bloom in early to midsummer and often rebloom. Deadhead and keep moist to encourage repeat flushes on this tidy edger.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall.

directions: Plant the crown an inch below grade and water in well. Large light yellow flowers with a green throat open over a long midsummer season on tall, branching scapes. Keep moist, feed in spring, and divide crowded clumps every few years.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Set the crown just below the surface and firm the soil around it. Soft melon-pink flowers with a green throat open in early to midsummer on tidy, well-branched scapes. Keep moist during establishment and divide every three years to sustain bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall for a long season.

directions: Plant this dwarf rebloomer with the crown level in the soil. Small clear yellow flowers repeat from early summer into fall on short scapes ideal for edging. Keep moist and deadhead to keep the repeat bloom coming.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall.

directions: Set the crown an inch below grade over spread roots and water in. Deep wine-red flowers with a darker eye and green throat bloom in midsummer on sturdy scapes. Keep evenly moist and divide crowded clumps every few years for reliable flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant the crown just below the surface and water in well. Near-white to pale cream flowers with a green throat give a cool look in the midsummer border. Keep moist through bloom and divide crowded clumps every few years to keep them flowering.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall.

directions: Set the crown an inch below grade and water thoroughly. Bright red flowers with a yellow-green throat bloom over a long midsummer season on sturdy scapes. Feed each spring, keep moist, and divide every three to four years to keep bloom heavy.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Set the crown just below the surface over firmed soil. Ruffled double yellow flowers give a full, layered look in early to midsummer on compact scapes. Keep moist during bloom and divide every few years to maintain the double display.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Tuck this tiny selection at the front of a bed with the crown level in the soil. Very small golden-yellow flowers bloom in early to midsummer on short scapes, perfect for edging and troughs. Keep moist and divide every few years to keep the little clumps vigorous.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall.

directions: Plant the crown an inch below grade and water in well. Soft pink flowers with a green throat open in midsummer on well-branched scapes. Keep evenly moist through bloom and divide crowded clumps every three to four years for best flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Spread the roots over firmed soil and keep the crown near the surface. Spidery pale cream-yellow flowers carry very long, narrow twisting segments on tall scapes in midsummer. Give steady moisture and divide every few years to keep the airy form.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Set the crown just below the surface and firm soil around it. Bright yellow flowers marked with a bold red eyezone open in early to midsummer on tidy scapes. Keep moist during bloom and divide every few years to keep the clump productive.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set roots over firmed soil with the crown near the surface. Large pale creamy-yellow flowers open in midsummer on tall, robust scapes well above the foliage. Water during dry weather and divide crowded clumps every three to four years.

planting.note: Set out divisions once the ground has thawed and soil can be worked.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown level with the soil surface in rich, humus-heavy ground. This compact, fast-multiplying mound forms tidy heart-shaped blue-green leaves and sends up lavender bells in midsummer. Keep soil evenly moist and mulch lightly to hold water during hot spells.

planting.note: Divide and replant in early spring as shoots begin to emerge.

directions: Site this cup-leaved variety in dappled shade where its blue-green centers and cream margins hold color. Set the crown at grade and firm the soil, then water in well. Pale lavender flowers rise on short scapes in early to midsummer above the dimpled foliage.

planting.note: Best moved in spring or early fall into moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Give this vigorous mound room to spread, planting the crown even with the soil in fertile ground. Its heavily corrugated green leaves carry creamy-white margins and form a broad dome. Near-white flowers appear in midsummer, and the leaves layer in a conical, tree-like silhouette that gives the plant its name.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once frost danger passes and soil warms.

directions: Set this large, arching mound where its wide creamy-yellow leaf margins can brighten a shady bed. Plant the crown at grade in rich, well-drained soil and keep consistently moist. Lavender flowers open on tall scapes in mid to late summer above the elegant two-toned foliage.

planting.note: Move and divide in spring, allowing several years to reach full stature.

directions: A stately variegated sport of Krossa Regal, this hosta forms an upright, vase-shaped clump of frosty blue-gray leaves edged in creamy gold. Plant the crown level with the soil and give it space to develop its full architecture. Tall lavender flower spikes tower well above the foliage in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; morning sun deepens the golden leaf color.

directions: This bright gold hosta tolerates more sun than most and colors best with a few hours of gentle morning light. Plant the crown at grade in rich soil and keep it well watered where exposed. The twisted, upright golden leaves form a fountain-like mound topped by lavender flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; spreads steadily to form a dense edging.

directions: Use this small, lance-leaved hosta as a shady-border edging, spacing divisions closely for a continuous ribbon. Set crowns level with the soil in moist, humus-rich ground. Deep green leaves with crisp white margins carry rich purple flowers on tall scapes in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to keep the gold centers vivid.

directions: Plant this award-winning hosta where filtered light brightens its golden leaf centers ringed with dark green. Set the crown at grade in fertile, moisture-holding soil and mulch to conserve water. Large, deeply corrugated leaves form a broad mound crowned by pale lavender flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in bright, dappled shade.

directions: This showy hosta unfurls streaky gold-centered leaves edged in irregular green that shift as the season progresses. Plant the crown level with the soil in rich, evenly moist ground. Lavender flowers rise above the mound in midsummer; give it filtered light to keep the bright variegation crisp.

planting.note: Plant in spring; upright habit reaches full size over a few seasons.

directions: This large, upright hosta forms a vase-shaped clump of thick gold-green leaves that brighten with light exposure. Plant the crown at grade in rich, well-drained soil and water regularly. Sturdy scapes carry pale lavender flowers well above the foliage in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to protect the white leaf centers.

directions: Grow this dramatic hosta in shade where its white-centered, streaky green-margined leaves resist scorching. Plant the crown even with the soil in moist, humus-rich ground. Lavender flowers appear in summer; the bold center variegation is best in cooler, sheltered spots.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in bright shade for best contrast.

directions: Prized for the narrow white flash separating its gold leaf center from the dark green margin, this hosta earns its distinctive name. Plant the crown at grade in fertile, moisture-retentive soil. Lavender flowers rise on tall scapes in midsummer above the striking layered foliage.
